speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Philip Katz. Philip, we were just talking about your father. Can you tell me something about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something is almost impossible because of the extraordinary personality of my mother. It would take a lifetime to describe her. But I will try to compress it and still give some kind of a picture of her. My mother was the daughter of the chief rabbi of [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On her mother's side, she was a descendent of-- at least we know from the early 16th century-- of the first chief rabbi of Buda. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was one of-- let's see-- one, two, three, four girls and two boys. My grandfather was a very young man when he passed away. He was very ill. I'm assuming because of the descriptions that he must have had cancer. And my mother, as a very young woman, was traveling with him from Budapest to Prague to Vienna to various experts and institutions that hopefully were going to cure him, which they didn't succeed, of course. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother's personal ambition all her life was to have become a doctor and was considered by the doctors who had met her because of her being the companion and nurse of my grandfather, apparently had unusual talents and capabilities in, in that direction. But of course, she was not permitted to go to school and in fact, was married off early, as were the other girls, so that my grandfather would be alive and live to see the marriages of his children. Her two brothers were both rabbis. In fact, the older brother-- I think he was the older brother-- had inherited his father's rabbinic seat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother, perhaps in a completely autodidact way, because she had-- I said she had no opportunity for schooling-- became a highly intelligent, extremely knowledgeable, and very literate intellectual, to the point where she literally maintained what perhaps in different circumstances and in a different age would have been called a salon. Our house became a kind of a, almost a debating society, where all the town's intellectuals congregated, literally sitting at her feet, and where intellectual [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had been flying day and night, virtually, which in and of itself, had a tremendous impact on me and on my sisters because obviously, we couldn't be kept out of it. We wouldn't, we wouldn't go to bed. And we wouldn't leave [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the room where this fiery debate and discussion of, of the world and all its ills were being discussed, analyzed, taken apart, and put back together. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother, who was obviously a completely observant Jew and wore a sheitel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was nonetheless [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] really the most sophisticated, and as far as we, the children, are concerned, certainly the most complete, the most completely human being that we have ever known, the most civilized and wisest of all people, as far as we are concerned. And this holds true for my sisters, too. She never died. We, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we sort of spend our life trying to measure up to what may be our idealized ideal of her and measure our lives and our actions to her standards to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Treza Halpert, T-R-E-Z-A, H-A-L-P-E-R-T. She had a tremendous impact not only on us but on, on the wider surroundings. Much sought her for her wise counsel and looked up to by everyone who, who certainly met her and by many who have only known her by reputation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think bequeathed to me, who [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at a certain point in her life was probably the closest [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to her because my father had been out of the house since 1939, having gone to America. And during those days, I had to assume the, the role of the man of the house, so I became, I think-- I'm sure that my sisters feel that way about their respective relationship-- but I think I became the closest collaborator of hers and closest friend of hers because there were areas of life that she didn't have to share with my sisters but had to share with me. I became something of a helper and breadwinner in the last three, four years before the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father get to the United States in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father finally-- because it became physically dangerous for him to attempt to continue in the United States-- I mean in Hungary-- finally permitted himself to be persuaded by my mother to go to the United States. Ostensibly, he came to represent Hungarian wine and liquor export to the World's Fair in 1939 at New York but with the intention of what he considered to weather the storm. Maybe it'll take six months or so. He had been to the United States before. And my mother, who sensed the-- way, way back-- the inevitability of the tragedy that was lurking at us and at the Jewish community of Hungary, was begging him for years to go to the United States. But of course, he wouldn't go, because as the saying went in Yiddish, in America you can't be a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he refused. But when it became physically impossible for him-- I say he came under this subterfuge, and then eventually, he realized that he, he may have to stay and, and eventually got stuck because the great power-hungry declared war on the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in fact, we were-- he finally managed to get immigration papers for us, and we were invited to come to the consulate in, in Budapest or the embassy. And-- but it was already too late. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] However, what I started to say was that my mother also imparted to me and, and endowed me with one of the most important, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] facts of my life. She herself managed to construct for herself a very unique kind of synthesis between her Judaism and her, and her humanitarianism. And she managed to, to live in these two worlds, as I have, thanks to her being able to live in, in this perhaps bipolar world without feeling the, any dichotomy. I feel myself perfectly integrated as a Jew and a human being without any either emotional or intellectual problems [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] resulting from being, so to say, one foot in, in the Jewish world and one foot in, in the world at large.

speaker: interviewer
text: What became of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Unfortunately, she, like so many of the Hungarian Jews, was murdered the day we arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have siblings. Can you tell me something about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've had five sisters, who were alive in 1944. I actually had family-- my mother had two twin boys who died at the age of three to six months. But there were six of us. I was the only boy. My sisters [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jolan, Berta, Izabella, and Regina, and Helen were all living at home in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the time of the, of the Holocaust. And all of them, when the Holocaust set in, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] really like each other, all-- but at the same time, of course, all different personalities and all of them striving to make themselves into the image of my mother, just like I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the eldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister Jolan was the eldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jolan Katz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We, we called her in the family [INAUDIBLE]. My sisters were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] elegant young women in, in town. They were kind of fashionable. And they were-- the whole family was a rather well-respected and well-known family. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, they, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they, too, were looked up to by their peers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in their case, too, as has been the case in my particular case, we had a certain following. We were almost all Pied Pipers in that sense. We, we all had the coterie of friends who, who sort of looked upon us as the trendsetters and the pacesetters. It, it wasn't only that we were friends, but we, we somehow-- we were, as I say, trendsetters for, for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for the record, Phillip, if you could list clearly the names of each sister and spell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jolan, as I have told you, J-O-L-A-N. She was the oldest, or is it the eldest? And then came Berta, B-E-R-T-A, who the nickname was Csicsa [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Hungarian. That is spelled C-S-I-C-S-A and means big mouth. She, she was and is a big mouth. And then came Izabella, who was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] known as Baby [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and in the family was, was called Madame Drama, because she had this dramatic flair, which she has retained for, for all her life. She's a, indeed, a very dramatic personality and, and incidentally, was not only dramatic in, in the sense of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] being very attractive. She's extremely beautiful and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] impressive, but she always wanted to be an actress and was fairly well-known in town for her impersonation of all sorts of actresses, particularly Joan Crawford. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she has retained that, that dramatic flair in, in everything, in her appearance. She can write a postcard and it, it's a big drama on a small postcard. Then came my sister Regina, who is R-E-G-I-N-A, and who was generally known by her Hebrew name with a diminutive. We either called her [? Rivka ?] or [? Rivkala, ?] usually. She, she was the only one who managed to go to junior high and-- but could never finish, because she was thrown out as a Jew in 1944 or '43. I don't really remember. She, too, was interested in being an actress. And I think I've shown you a picture of one of the plays that she has participated in. And incidentally, I should tell you that my sister Berta was at one time considered one of the potentially great poetesses of Hungary. And, and interestingly enough, she ceased to write when she came to, to the United States. The change of language and culture apparently-- and apparently, you know, robbed her of, of the, the vehicle to express herself, on the one hand. And on the other hand, she, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she got married virtually 10 days after she arrived in the United States. And she became a housewife and a mother. And peculiarly enough, my sister Izabella, who was the performer, so to say, eventually became the writer and has published several books on the Holocaust, including a children's book for ages eight and nine, which is supposed to be the only one directed at, so far, at that age level and has been a tremendous success. Her others, two books, have been translated into half a dozen languages and certainly part of every Holocaust course and library. My sister Regina was always sought out for her recitation of poetry, even to the point where, in fairly dark days already, at the Hungarian national independence day, I suppose you would call it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] despite the fact that she was a Jew, she was invited to recite the great national hymn or, or anthem at the public observance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Reciting poetry was, in those days in Hungary, as in other parts of Europe, was considered a great art.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your youngest sibling?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] That is probably the most difficult for myself and, and for my sisters. My youngest sister was about 13 or going on 13 when we arrived in Auschwitz and was sent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to her death with my mother. It has been probably the most difficult thing for us to endure because having been the youngest, we all doted on her. We all, each and every one of us, we always catch ourselves talking and thinking and feeling about her as though she was our child, every one of us. We each claim, so to say, to be the parent. And she was an extraordinarily beautiful and sensitive [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] girl. And we all poured our whole existence into her. We all saw in her the incarnation of the best that we had. And all our hopes and, and aspirations were concentrated and distilled in her. We, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] perhaps unconsciously, hoped that whatever fate befell us, she we will see a better day. She will live in a more human world, in a more humane world. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That she was lost is somehow the greatest loss to us because she was the quintessential [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] impersonation and, and, and incarnation of all that we were and all that we wanted to be and all that we hope to be and all that we hoped for for the Jews, for humanity, and for the world. It's extremely difficult for us to this day [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, in the first place, see her as-- right now, she would be a woman in her late 50s or something like that, or 60s. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We, of course, always see her as, as we saw her then, and we can't make our peace with her not being. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Perhaps it's the same with other families, too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To us, she was all. And in a very real sense, she really still is. We don't exist without her, because she exists and will for us always [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a very real way, the same as my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip, can you take me to the days before the ghetto was established in Kisvarda? What was happening? What did you see? What were people talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, everybody was-- just the town was in an uproar. People were wrought up, completely bewildered, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] really without council. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The leadership of the community, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a very broad sense and certainly in a historic sense, has failed [INAUDIBLE]. Jewish leadership [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has failed or-- perhaps in large measure as a result of circumstances or not because of their own fault. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They were paralyzed. And they, by and large, insisted on hanging on to these most unrealistic and impractical notion that the community and the city was going to be unharmed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one of the sinful results of it was that it was difficult to get [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] help for those who were already, in effect, in the ghetto-- although they were then moved into another location in, in, in what became the ghetto-- until virtually the last day. I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to this day-- I have all sorts of difficulties with this. I remember a former president of the community that I went to and asked him to give me money so I could buy food and, and clothing or whatever for the people who had been brought in before we were put into the ghetto. And he virtually threw me out. In a way, it's, it's quite understandable, you know. They-- aside from the fact that they, they could not cope with-- they couldn't expect it to be-- to cope with the events. It was, it was not conceivable for them. And so you simply couldn't be prepared for it. And aside from the fact that there was a total dislocation and disorientation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it's-- aside from all of that, there was this, this idee fixe that they could somehow save themselves [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the day that were, that was set for us to be-- or the next morning-- for us to be put into the ghetto, that poor man ran after me with literally a basket full of cash. It finally sank in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's a very painful thing, though, you know, no one should blame anyone. The events and the circumstances were so completely beyond the human ken that even in hindsight, it would be difficult to prescribe what would be the right behavior, what would be the right action to take or the right, the right advice to give.

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip, we're going to take a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.
